intro: Voluntary muscles attach to the skeleton to create movement. They work by contracting (shortening).
notes: Muscle Belly: The thick, fleshy part that contracts.
Tendon: Tough fibers that join Muscle to Bone.
Origin: Attachment to the stationary bone (e.g., Shoulder).
Insertion: Attachment to the moving bone (e.g., Forearm).

hotspots.label: Biceps (Flexor)
hotspots.description:
The Biceps is the muscle located at the front of the upper arm.

When it contracts (becomes short and thick), it pulls the radius bone to bend the arm at the elbow.
hotspots.examTip: It is called a Flexor muscle because it flexes (bends) the joint.

hotspots.label: Triceps (Extensor)
hotspots.description:
The Triceps is located at the back of the upper arm.

When it contracts, it pulls the ulna bone to straighten the arm.
hotspots.examTip: It is called an Extensor because it extends (straightens) the joint. Remember: Biceps and Triceps are an Antagonistic Pair (they work in opposites).

hotspots.label: Tendon
hotspots.description:
Tendons are tough, non-elastic white cords.

Why non-elastic? If they stretched like rubber bands, the muscle would contract but the bone wouldn't move! They must be stiff to transfer the force.

hotspots.label: Origin Point
hotspots.description:
Muscles are attached to bones at two points:
1. Origin: Where the muscle joins the bone that does not move (here, the Humerus/Scapula).
2. Insertion: Where it joins the bone that moves (the forearm).
hotspots.examTip: Muscles always pull the Insertion towards the Origin.
